## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action** — including clarifying questions, exploring the codebase, or checking files. If it turns out wrong for the situation, you don't have to use it.

**Before entering plan mode:** if you haven't already brainstormed, invoke the brainstorming skill first.

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills apply, process skills come first — they set the approach, then implementation skills (frontend-design, etc.) carry it out. Brainstorming and systematic-debugging are Superpowers' most common process skills, but the rule holds for any of them.

- "Let's build X" → superpowers:brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
- "Fix this bug" → superpowers:systematic-debugging first, then domain skills.

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

- Antigravity: `references/antigravity-tools.md`

## User Instructions

</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>"

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-dev:code-architect: Designs feature architectures by analyzing existing codebase patterns and conventions, then providing comprehensive implementation blueprints with specific files to create/modify, component designs, data flows, and build sequences (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-explorer: Deeply analyzes existing codebase features by tracing execution paths, mapping architecture layers, understanding patterns and abstractions, and documenting dependencies to inform new development (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-reviewer: Reviews code for bugs, logic errors, security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and adherence to project conventions, using confidence-based filtering to report only high-priority issues that truly matter (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- sonarqube:sonarqube-reviewer: Reviews code changes against SonarQube quality, security, and dependency-risk standards by composing this plugin's sonar-* skills. Use proactively before merging a PR or when the user asks for a SonarQube-based code review, a second opinion on code quality, or to "review my changes with SonarQube." (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash(git diff:*), Bash(git status:*), Bash(git branch:*), Bash(git symbolic-ref:*), Bash(sonar:*), mcp__sonarqube__*, Skill)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## plugin:github:github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").

Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/kevinbarnard/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/6.1.1/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer the visual companion just-in-time** — NOT upfront. The first time a question would genuinely be clearer shown than described, offer it then (its own message); on approval its browser tab opens for you. If no visual question ever arises, never offer it. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion (just-in-time):** Do NOT offer it upfront. Wait until a question would genuinely be clearer shown than told — a real mockup / layout / diagram question, not merely a UI *topic*. The first time that happens, offer it then, as its own message:
> "This next part might be easier if I show you — I can put together mockups, diagrams, and comparisons in a browser tab as we go. It's still new and can be token-intensive. Want me to? I'll open it for you."

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Only the offer — no clarifying question, summary, or other content. Wait for the user's response. If they accept, start the server with `--open` so their browser opens to the first screen automatically. If they decline, continue text-only and don't offer again unless they raise it.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

input.description: Explore minixrs CI/QEMU/gating
input.prompt:
Explore the repo at /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs (medium thoroughness). I'm planning a "CI QEMU smoke test" chunk from the Phase 5 prep tracker, possibly using a blog_os-style custom test framework, and want to understand current state. Report back with file paths and concrete excerpts (short) for:

1. docs/plans/phase-5-prep.md — the full content, especially any chunk describing a CI QEMU smoke test (its exact wording, scope, markers like "◀ next"). Also the Phase 5 section of docs/plan.md if it mentions this.
2. .github/workflows/ci.yml — job structure: names of jobs, runner labels (ubuntu-latest? arm?), toolchain install steps, how the pinned nightly from rust-toolchain.toml is used, whether QEMU is installed anywhere, roughly how long-running jobs are structured. Also rust-toolchain.toml content.
3. tools/qemu-run.sh — full content (it's the cargo runner for the kernel). And the cargo runner wiring: .cargo/config.toml (or config) — runner setting, target, build-std flags, unstable flags, aliases like `kernel-aarch64`.
4. kernel/src/main.rs — the overall structure: the `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` gating pattern (how many modules are gated, is there a single gate or per-module), the panic handler, entry point, and how `boot_image/mod.rs`'s include_bytes/env!("BOOT_IMAGE_PATH") gate works. Report the actual gating shape (e.g., one big `#[cfg]` block vs attribute per module).
5. What the kernel prints on a successful boot that a smoke test could assert on: search kernel sources for distinctive boot-complete / trace strings (e.g. "[as]", "[ksys", "[alarm", "[pf]", panic messages, "el0_sync_unexpected"). Identify 3-5 strings that indicate (a) healthy boot, (b) failure. Check how the QEMU serial console output is produced (UART driver path).
6. kernel/build.rs — how it builds servers, what env vars it needs, anything that would break under `cargo test --target aarch64-unknown-none` or a second binary/test harness (e.g., does it gate on CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS?).
7. Whether there is any existing test infrastructure targeting QEMU (grep for "smoke", "test_case", "custom_test_frameworks", "semihosting", "psci", "SYSTEM_OFF" in the repo). Also check how the kernel currently shuts down / idles (does it ever exit QEMU? PSCI usage?).

Return a structured summary; keep excerpts brief but exact.

input.description: Explore blog_os test framework
input.prompt:
Explore the repo at /Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/blog_os (Philipp Oppermann's blog_os tutorial checkout; medium thoroughness). Note which branch/post the checkout is on (git branch/log -1). I want a precise description of its custom testing framework so I can adapt it to an aarch64 (QEMU virt) Rust microkernel. Report with exact file paths and short excerpts:

1. The `custom_test_frameworks` setup in src/main.rs and/or src/lib.rs: the feature attributes (`#![feature(custom_test_frameworks)]`, `#![test_runner(...)]`, `#![reexport_test_harness_main = ...]`), the `test_runner` function, the `Testable` trait pattern, and how `#[test_case]` tests are declared.
2. How tests exit QEMU: the `QemuExitCode` enum and `exit_qemu` function (isa-debug-exit port I/O), and the Cargo.toml / config metadata that wires the exit device and success-exit-code (e.g. `package.metadata.bootimage` `test-args`, `test-success-exit-code`, `test-timeout`).
3. How test output reaches the host: serial (uart_16550) setup, `serial_print!` macros, and the `-serial stdio` / `-display none` QEMU args.
4. How `cargo test` maps to QEMU runs: the runner configuration (bootimage runner in .cargo/config.toml), how integration tests in `tests/` each become a separate QEMU boot (e.g. tests/should_panic.rs, tests/basic_boot.rs — harness = false cases), and how the panic handler differs in test vs normal mode (`#[cfg(test)]` panic handler pattern).
5. The split between main.rs and lib.rs that lets the kernel be tested: what lives in lib.rs, how `#[cfg(test)]` entry point works in lib.rs.
6. List everything that is x86_64-specific in this scheme (isa-debug-exit port 0xf4, uart_16550, bootimage tool, BIOS boot) — i.e., the parts that would need aarch64 replacements.

Return a structured summary with exact code excerpts for the key attributes/functions (keep each excerpt under ~15 lines).

input.description: Research aarch64 QEMU test exit + CI
input.prompt:
Web-research task (use WebSearch/WebFetch; no code changes). I'm designing a CI smoke/test harness for a Rust aarch64 bare-metal kernel (no_std, QEMU `-M virt`, EL1, pinned dated nightly toolchain) on GitHub Actions, adapting blog_os's custom_test_frameworks approach (which uses x86 isa-debug-exit). Research and report on:

1. **Exiting qemu-system-aarch64 with a controllable exit code from guest EL1 code**: the semihosting approach — QEMU `-semihosting` (or `-semihosting-config`) plus the aarch64 semihosting call (`HLT #0xF000`, W0=0x18 SYS_EXIT / ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit with the extended 64-bit parameter block for exit codes). Confirm: exact instruction/register convention for aarch64 (not arm32 SVC), how QEMU maps ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit to host exit code, whether nonzero exit codes work (`-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native` + SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED or the field2 code), and any gotchas (semihosting enabled lets guest read host files — CI security irrelevance, but note `-semihosting` also affects other instructions?). Also the alternative: PSCI SYSTEM_OFF via HVC/SMC on `-M virt` (clean shutdown but always exit code 0), and `virt` device `sysbus-debug-exit`? (does qemu virt machine have an equivalent of isa-debug-exit — I believe not; confirm). Also mention the `qemu -no-reboot`/`-no-shutdown` interplay if relevant.
2. **Rust `custom_test_frameworks` feature status in 2025/2026 nightlies**: is `#![feature(custom_test_frameworks)]` still available on current nightly? Any deprecation/removal discussion (RFC/issue status, e.g. rust-lang/rust#50297)? Does it still work with `#![no_std] #![no_main]` + `#![reexport_test_harness_main]`?
3. **GitHub Actions runners for this**: availability/pricing of `ubuntu-24.04-arm` (arm64) hosted runners for public repos (free?), whether they support KVM (/dev/kvm) for qemu-system-aarch64 acceleration, and what qemu-system-aarch64 version ships in ubuntu-24.04 apt (is it new enough for `-M virt` semihosting exit codes — basically any modern QEMU is). Also: on plain ubuntu-latest x86 runners, qemu-system-aarch64 runs under TCG — typical slowdown, and whether people successfully run aarch64 kernel boot tests this way (examples: Rust OS projects, rust-osdev).
4. **Prior art**: any known aarch64 no_std Rust projects doing `cargo test` → QEMU with semihosting exit (e.g. the `qemu-exit` crate — does it support aarch64? maintained? license — must be permissive/BSD-compatible, no GPL), `semihosting` crate (taiki-e), rust-embedded `cortex-m-semihosting` equivalent for aarch64.

Report findings with sources/URLs, flagging anything uncertain.

# blog_os Custom Testing Framework — Analysis

**Checkout state:** branch `main`, HEAD = `e45c8a4 post09` (Post 09 "Paging Implementation"; the testing framework was introduced in Post 04 "Testing" and is fully present). Working tree clean except untracked `.idea/`. Edition 2024, `nightly` toolchain.

Key files: `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/blog_os/src/lib.rs`, `/src/main.rs`, `/src/serial.rs`, `/Cargo.toml`, `/.cargo/config.toml`, `/x86_64-blog_os.json`, and `/tests/{basic_boot,should_panic,stack_overflow}.rs`.

---

## 1. `custom_test_frameworks` setup

Both `src/lib.rs` and `src/main.rs` (and each harnessed integration test) carry the same crate-attribute block. From `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/blog_os/src/lib.rs:1-6`:

```rust
#![no_std]
#![cfg_attr(test, no_main)]        // library binary needs its own entry only under `cargo test`
#![feature(custom_test_frameworks)]
#![feature(abi_x86_interrupt)]
#![test_runner(crate::test_runner)]
#![reexport_test_harness_main = "test_main"]
```

- `custom_test_frameworks` replaces the std `test` crate (unavailable on `no_std`).
- `test_runner(...)` names the function the compiler calls with the collected tests.
- `reexport_test_harness_main = "test_main"` renames the generated harness entry from `main` to `test_main`, so it can be called manually from a `no_main` kernel entry point.

In `main.rs:4` the runner path is `blog_os::test_runner` (points at the lib). Note `cfg_attr(test, no_main)` in lib.rs: the library only becomes `no_main` when compiled as a test binary.

**`Testable` trait pattern** (`src/lib.rs:23-44`) — gives each test a name-printing wrapper via a blanket impl over all `Fn()`:

```rust
pub trait Testable {
    fn run(&self) -> ();
}
impl<T> Testable for T where T: Fn() {
    fn run(&self) {
        serial_print!("{}...\t", core::any::type_name::<T>());
        self();
        serial_println!("[ok]");
    }
}
pub fn test_runner(tests: &[&dyn Testable]) {
    serial_println!("Running {} tests", tests.len());
    for test in tests { test.run(); }
    exit_qemu(QemuExitCode::Success);
}
```

**`#[test_case]` declaration** — the framework collects any fn marked `#[test_case]`. Example in `src/main.rs:55-58`:

```rust
#[test_case]
fn trivial_assertion() {
    assert_eq!(1, 1);
}
```

The compiler builds a slice of `&dyn Testable` from all `#[test_case]` items and passes it to `test_runner`.

---

## 2. Exiting QEMU (`QemuExitCode` + `exit_qemu`)

`src/lib.rs:53-67` — writes to the `isa-debug-exit` device's I/O port `0xf4`:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
#[repr(u32)]
pub enum QemuExitCode {
    Success = 0x10,
    Failed  = 0x11,
}
pub fn exit_qemu(exit_code: QemuExitCode) {
    use x86_64::instructions::port::Port;
    unsafe {
        let mut port = Port::new(0xf4);
        port.write(exit_code as u32);
    }
}
```

Mechanism: the `isa-debug-exit` device causes QEMU to exit with process status `(value << 1) | 1`. So `Success = 0x10` → host exit code `(0x10<<1)|1 = 33`.

**Cargo metadata wiring** (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/blog_os/Cargo.toml:19-29`), consumed by the `bootimage` tool:

```toml
[package.metadata.bootimage]
test-args = [
    "-device", "isa-debug-exit,iobase=0xf4,iosize=0x04",
    "-serial", "stdio",
    "-display", "none",
]
test-timeout = 300
test-success-exit-code = 33
```

`test-success-exit-code = 33` tells bootimage that host code 33 (from `QemuExitCode::Success`) means the test suite passed; anything else is a failure. `iobase=0xf4,iosize=0x04` matches the `Port::new(0xf4)` / `u32` write.

---

## 3. Test output to host — serial (UART 16550)

`src/serial.rs:1-11` sets up COM1 at I/O port `0x3F8` via the `uart_16550` crate:

```rust
use uart_16550::SerialPort;
lazy_static! {
    pub static ref SERIAL1: Mutex<SerialPort> = {
        let mut serial_port = unsafe { SerialPort::new(0x3F8) };
        serial_port.init();
        Mutex::new(serial_port)
    };
}
```

`_print` (serial.rs:13-24) locks `SERIAL1` and writes, wrapped in `x86_64 ... interrupts::without_interrupts(...)` to avoid deadlock. Macros `serial_print!` / `serial_println!` (serial.rs:27-41) are `#[macro_export]`ed and forward to `$crate::serial::_print(format_args!(...))`.

The QEMU args `-serial stdio` redirect the guest's COM1 output to the host terminal's stdout, and `-display none` suppresses the graphical window (both in the `test-args` above), so all `serial_println!` output from `test_runner`/`Testable::run` appears on the host running `cargo test`.

---

## 4. `cargo test` → QEMU mapping

**Runner** — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/blog_os/.cargo/config.toml`:

```toml
[build]
target = "x86_64-blog_os.json"

[target.'cfg(target_os = "none")']
runner = "bootimage runner"
```

Flow: `cargo test` compiles each test binary for the bare-metal target, then invokes `bootimage runner <binary>` per binary. `bootimage` links the compiled kernel with the BIOS `bootloader` crate into a bootable disk image and launches QEMU, appending the `test-args`. QEMU boots the image; the kernel runs `test_main()` and exits via port `0xf4`. bootimage compares QEMU's host exit code to `test-success-exit-code = 33`.

**Each `tests/*.rs` = a separate QEMU boot.** Every integration test is its own `#![no_main]` binary with its own `_start`/entry, so it produces a separate bootable image and separate QEMU run.

- Standard harnessed test — `tests/basic_boot.rs:1-25`: repeats the full crate-attribute block, defines `_start` calling `test_main()`, and uses `#[test_case]`. Its panic handler delegates to `blog_os::test_panic_handler`.
- **`harness = false` tests** — declared in `Cargo.toml:36-42`:

```toml
[[test]]
name = "should_panic"
harness = false
[[test]]
name = "stack_overflow"
harness = false
```

These opt out of the custom test-framework harness entirely (no `test_main`, no `#[test_case]` collection). `tests/should_panic.rs` has no framework attributes at all; its `_start` calls one hard-coded `should_fail()` and the **panic handler is the success path** (panic → `[ok]` → `exit_qemu(Success)`; no panic → `exit_qemu(Failed)`). `tests/stack_overflow.rs` similarly hand-codes one scenario, installing a test IDT whose double-fault handler reports success. `harness = false` is required whenever the "pass" condition is a panic/fault, since a normal harness would treat any panic as failure.

**Panic handler differs by mode** (`src/main.rs:41-53`):

```rust
#[cfg(not(test))]
#[panic_handler]
fn panic(info: &PanicInfo) -> ! {
    println!("{}", info);            // normal: print to VGA, halt
    blog_os::hlt_loop();
}
#[cfg(test)]
#[panic_handler]
fn panic(info: &PanicInfo) -> ! {
    blog_os::test_panic_handler(info) // test: serial "[failed]" + exit_qemu(Failed)
}
```

`test_panic_handler` (lib.rs:46-51) prints `[failed]` + the error over serial and calls `exit_qemu(QemuExitCode::Failed)`, so a failing assertion produces a non-33 exit code that bootimage reports as a failure.

---

## 5. main.rs / lib.rs split for testability

- **lib.rs is the actual library**: it holds all reusable pieces — `init()`, the whole test framework (`Testable`, `test_runner`, `test_panic_handler`, `QemuExitCode`, `exit_qemu`, `hlt_loop`) and the modules (`gdt`, `interrupts`, `memory`, `serial`, `vga_buffer`). Because it's a `#[no_std]` library crate, both `main.rs` and every `tests/*.rs` can `use blog_os::...`.
- **main.rs is a thin binary**: its `kernal_main` does real boot work and, under test, calls `test_main()` (`main.rs:34-35`).
- **`#[cfg(test)]` entry point inside lib.rs** (`src/lib.rs:75-92`) lets the library be tested *on its own* (`cargo test --lib`) as a bootable kernel. Only when compiled as a test does it become `no_main` (line 2) and define an entry:

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
use bootloader::{BootInfo, entry_point};
#[cfg(test)]
entry_point!(test_kernel_main);
#[cfg(test)]
pub fn test_kernel_main(_boot_info: &'static BootInfo) -> ! {
    init();
    test_main();
    hlt_loop();
}
#[cfg(test)]
#[panic_handler]
fn panic(info: &PanicInfo) -> ! { test_panic_handler(info) }
```

So the lib supplies its own `_start` (via `entry_point!`) and panic handler only under `cfg(test)`; in normal builds those come from `main.rs`.

---

## 6. x86_64-specific parts (need aarch64 / QEMU `virt` replacements)

| Piece | Where | x86-specific detail | aarch64 replacement direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **isa-debug-exit port I/O** | `lib.rs:60-67`; `Cargo.toml:21` | Port-mapped I/O `Port::new(0xf4)` + QEMU `-device isa-debug-exit,iobase=0xf4`. ISA/x86 only. | aarch64 `virt` has no ISA ports. Use the **`semihosting`** exit (`SYS_EXIT` via `hlt #0xF000` / the `semihosting` crate) with QEMU `-semihosting`, or write to the **PL061/SP804 or the `sysreset`/PSCI** path. Common choice: ARM semihosting `sys_exit`. |
| **`test-success-exit-code = 33`** | `Cargo.toml:29` | Derived from x86 isa-debug-exit `(v<<1)|1` formula. | Recompute for your chosen exit mechanism (semihosting returns your code directly, so success code would just be your `Success` value). |
| **UART / serial** | `serial.rs:1-11`; QEMU `-serial stdio` | `uart_16550::SerialPort` at ISA port `0x3F8` (16550 UART, port I/O). | QEMU `virt` uses a **PL011** UART (MMIO at `0x0900_0000`). Replace `uart_16550` with a PL011 MMIO driver (or the `arm-pl011-uart` crate). `-serial stdio` still works. |
| **`bootimage` + `bootloader 0.9` (BIOS)** | `.cargo/config.toml` runner; `Cargo.toml:7` | `bootimage runner` builds a **BIOS/MBR x86 boot image**; `entry_point!`/`BootInfo` come from the x86 `bootloader` crate. | aarch64 QEMU `virt` loads an ELF/flat kernel directly via `qemu-system-aarch64 -M virt -kernel ...`. Replace the runner with a script/`runner = "qemu-system-aarch64 ..."` line; drop `bootloader`/`bootimage`; write your own `_start` and boot info handling. |
| **`entry_point!` / `BootInfo`** | `lib.rs:76-82`; `main.rs:8-13` | From x86 `bootloader` crate (`physical_memory_offset`, `memory_map`). | Define your own entry (e.g. `#[no_mangle] extern "C" fn _start`) and parse the **device tree** QEMU passes in `x0`. |
| **Custom target JSON** | `x86_64-blog_os.json` | `llvm-target: x86_64-unknown-none`, `arch: x86_64`, `-mmx,-sse,+soft-float`, `disable-redzone`. | Use `aarch64-unknown-none` (a built-in target, often no custom JSON needed) or an aarch64 JSON; feature flags differ. |
| **`feature(abi_x86_interrupt)` / `extern "x86-interrupt"`** | `lib.rs:4`; `tests/stack_overflow.rs` IDT/double-fault | x86 IDT + interrupt calling convention. | aarch64 uses a **vector table (VBAR_EL1)** with exception handlers; the `stack_overflow` test's IDT logic must be rewritten for AArch64 exception vectors. |
| **`x86_64` crate helpers** | `lib.rs:20,61,71`; `serial.rs:16` | `x86_64::instructions::{hlt, interrupts, port}` — port I/O, `hlt`, `cli/sti`. | aarch64: `wfi`/`wfe` for halt, `DAIF`/`msr daifset` for interrupt masking, MMIO instead of port I/O (use `cortex-a`/`aarch64-cpu` crate). |

**Framework parts that port unchanged (arch-neutral):** the `custom_test_frameworks` attribute block, the `Testable` trait + blanket impl, `test_runner`'s structure, the `#[cfg(test)]`/`#[cfg(not(test))]` panic-handler split, the `harness = false` pattern for panic/fault tests, and the main.rs/lib.rs split. Only the three primitives underneath — `exit_qemu`, the serial `_print`, and the entry point/panic wiring — need aarch64 bodies.</result>

**Framework parts that port unchanged (arch-neutral):** the `custom_test_frameworks` attribute block, the `Testable` trait + blanket impl, `test_runner`'s structure, the `#[cfg(test)]`/`#[cfg(not(test))]` panic-handler split, the `harness = false` pattern for panic/fault tests, and the main.rs/lib.rs split. # minix.rs — CI QEMU Smoke Test Prep: Current State

All paths absolute. Servers run at EL0 with no console, so **every runtime trace comes from the kernel-side PL011 UART** — the smoke test asserts on kernel traces, not server output.

## 1. Phase 5 prep tracker & plan

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/docs/plans/phase-5-prep.md`** — 6 chunks, all currently `◀ next`. **Chunk 1 is the CI QEMU smoke job** (lines 16-41). Exact scope wording:

- New job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (ubuntu): install `qemu-system-aarch64` + edk2 firmware, build via `cargo kernel-aarch64`, boot the cargo runner under `timeout 25`, capture serial log to a file.
- Grep log with `grep -a` (raw tick bytes make it binary-ish) against a checked-in expected-substrings file (suggested `tests/qemu-boot.expected`): "the 11 `[as]` lines, init's `SYS_FORK`/`SYS_EXEC name=worker`/`SYS_EXIT` chain, stub D's designed `sig=11` kill chain, RS `[alarm]` fires; and assert the absence of `panic` / `el0_sync_unexpected`."
- Start `continue-on-error: true` like geiger/miri; flip to blocking after a few stable PRs.
- Note: "QEMU-under-TCG guest time runs slower than wall clock — pick expectations that land well inside the timeout (boot chatter + first alarm fires, not tick counts)."
- Pin third-party actions to full commit SHAs.
- **Proof:** a PR that breaks boot fails the job; green run greps every marker.

Note (line 137, chunk 6): expected Phase 5 opening slice includes "fault-safe user copy for messages (`EFAULT`, not a kernel panic, on a bad user pointer)" — relevant if the smoke test ever exercises bad pointers.

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/docs/plan.md`**: line 492 `### Pre-Phase-5 cleanup ◀ next`; line 495 lists "a CI QEMU smoke job" among the chunks. Phase 5 section at line 502 (`### Phase 5: musl Fork + File Systems`) — still 6 bullets + milestone "C 'Hello World' compiled against musl runs". Verification strategy line 577: "Phase 5: `printf("Hello\n")` from C program reaches serial."

## 2. CI workflow & toolchain

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/.github/workflows/ci.yml`** — 8 jobs, **all `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`, no ARM runners**:

| Job | Notes |
|-----|-------|
| `fmt` | `cargo fmt --all --check` |
| `clippy` | `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` |
| `audit` | cargo-audit |
| `deny` | cargo-deny |
| `geiger` | **`continue-on-error: true`** (advisory) — the pattern chunk 1 wants to copy |
| `miri` | **`continue-on-error: true`** (advisory), tests only `-p ...kernel-shared -p ...vm -p ...pm` |
| `coverage` | cargo-llvm-cov → uploads `lcov.info` artifact |
| `sonar` | `needs: coverage`, gated on non-fork |

- **Toolchain install:** every real-build job runs `- name: Install pinned toolchain / run: rustup show`. `rustup show` reads `rust-toolchain.toml` and installs the pinned nightly + components + targets. Cache via `Swatinem/rust-cache@e18b497...`.
- **QEMU is NOT installed anywhere** in CI. No boot/kernel job exists — the smoke job is greenfield.
- All actions pinned to full SHAs (e.g. `actions/checkout@df4cb1c...`), matching chunk 1's convention.

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/rust-toolchain.toml`**: `channel = "nightly-2026-05-18"`, components `rust-src, rustfmt, clippy, llvm-tools, rust-analyzer`, targets `aarch64-unknown-none`, `x86_64-unknown-none`. (Chunk 5 flags this pin as ~2 months old — bump before Phase 5.)

## 3. Cargo runner wiring

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/tools/qemu-run.sh`** (cargo runner, invoked with kernel ELF as `$1`):
- Requires **Limine** `external/limine/dist/BOOTAA64.EFI` (auto-runs `make -C external/limine` if missing) and an **edk2/OVMF aarch64 firmware** (searches `QEMU_EFI_AARCH64`, brew paths, `/usr/share/edk2-aarch64/QEMU_EFI.fd`, `/usr/share/AAVMF/AAVMF_CODE.fd`).
- Stages an ESP dir (`target/esp`) with Limine + `tools/limine.conf` + the kernel, hands it to QEMU as a FAT drive.
- Launch line: `qemu-system-aarch64 -M "virt,gic-version=3" -cpu cortex-a72 -m 256M -bios "$FIRMWARE" -drive "file=fat:rw:fat-type=32:$ESP,format=raw,if=virtio" -display none -serial stdio -no-reboot "$@"`.
- **CI implication:** on ubuntu, need `qemu-system-arm` pkg + an edk2 firmware path (`/usr/share/AAVMF/AAVMF_CODE.fd` from `qemu-efi-aarch64`), plus the Limine binary (`make -C external/limine`). `external/limine` is a submodule/checkout not present in the tracked tree listing — verify it's fetched in CI.

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/.cargo/config.toml`**:
```
[target.aarch64-unknown-none]
runner = "tools/qemu-run.sh"
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=-Tkernel/src/arch/aarch64/linker.ld"]
[alias]
kernel-aarch64 = "build -p minixrs-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release"
```
No `build-std` / `-Zbuild-std` flags anywhere — the two `-unknown-none` targets are builtin (build.rs and `rust-toolchain.toml` rely on that). `cargo run -p minixrs-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release` invokes the runner; `cargo kernel-aarch64` only *builds*.

## 4. `kernel/src/main.rs` gating shape

**Per-item `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` attributes, NOT one big block.** Structure (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/main.rs`):
- Crate-level: `#![cfg_attr(target_os = "none", no_std)]` + `#![cfg_attr(target_os = "none", no_main)]`.
- A separate `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` attribute on **each of 10 modules** (`arch, boot_image, clock, ipc, mm, panic, proc, system, uart`), and again on `use core::fmt::Write`, `const TICK_HZ`, and `extern "C" fn kmain`.
- Entry point: `#[unsafe(no_mangle)] extern "C" fn kmain() -> !` (referenced by linker `ENTRY`, not called from Rust).
- Host fallback: `#[cfg(not(target_os = "none"))] fn main() {}` — collapses to a no-op so `cargo check --workspace` on host works.

**Panic handler** (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/panic.rs`): `#[panic_handler]` prints `\n!!! KERNEL PANIC: {info}` to a fresh `arch::Uart`, then `arch::halt()` (WFE loop).

**boot_image gate** (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/boot_image/mod.rs`): `static BOOT_IMAGE: &[u8] = include_bytes!(env!("BOOT_IMAGE_PATH"));` (line 34). The module-level doc comment (lines 25-27) explicitly states the whole module is gated on `target_os = "none"` in main.rs, so `env!("BOOT_IMAGE_PATH")` is only evaluated for the bare-metal build — host `cargo check`/`cargo test` (env var unset) never compile it. **The gate is the single `#[cfg]` on `mod boot_image` in main.rs, not an inner attribute.**

## 5. Boot-complete / failure strings for assertions

Servers cannot print (EL0, no console per CLAUDE.md:143). All strings below are kernel-emitted on the PL011 UART.

**UART path:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/uart.rs` re-exports `crate::arch::Uart`; on aarch64 that is `Pl011` (`arch/aarch64/mod.rs`: `pub use uart::Pl011 as Uart`), MMIO at `PL011_PHYS_BASE` (mapped via HHDM). Output goes to `-serial stdio`.

**Healthy-boot markers (assert presence):**
- `minix.rs booting on aarch64` — very first line (`main.rs:54`).
- `[as] {name} nr= ... ttbr0_pa= ... asid= ... entry=` — one per loaded address space; **11 `[as]` lines** on a good boot (`arch/aarch64/userland.rs:380` servers/init, `:691` stubs). CLAUDE.md:135 confirms: vm/ds/vfs/sched/rs/pm/init asid 1-7, stubs A-D asid 8-11.
- `[ksys SYS_FORK] parent= ... child_nr=15 ...` → `[ksys SYS_EXEC] target=15 name=worker ...` → `[ksys SYS_EXIT] target= ... nr=15 freed= ...` — the init→worker chain (`system/do_fork.rs:191`, `do_exec.rs:171`, `do_exit.rs:125`).
- `[alarm {n}] owner= ... nr= ... at= ...` — RS heartbeat alarm fires (`ipc/mod.rs:390`); CLAUDE.md notes "six RS `[alarm]` fires."
- `[ipc {n}]`, `[pf] proc= ... -> VM`, `[noq {n}]` — other liveness traces (`ipc/mod.rs:125`, `exception.rs:213`, `sched/...:399`).
- All healthy `[ksys …]` traces end `result=0` (CLAUDE.md:135: "every `result=0`").

**Designed (NOT failure) — must be tolerated:** `[ksys SYS_KILL] target= ... sig=11 -> pm` (`system/do_sig.rs:99`) — stub D's intentional SIGSEGV kill chain. The expected file should whitelist `sig=11`.

**Failure markers (assert absence):**
- `!!! KERNEL PANIC:` (`panic.rs:15`).
- `el0_sync_unexpected` / `!!! unexpected EL0 sync exception (not SVC)` (`arch/aarch64/exception.rs:86,114`; also panics with `EL0 sync exception: EC=... ESR_EL1=...` at :122).
- `!!! EL0 instruction abort` / `!!! EL0 data abort` (`exception.rs:95,103`).
- `do_irq: unexpected INTID` (`arch/aarch64/irq.rs:49`).

**Trace sampling caveat (important for choosing assertions):** most traces are *sampled*, not per-call. `[ipc N]` and `[ksys N]` use modulo-100 (`system/mod.rs:78` `KCALL_TRACE_EVERY=100`); several have a head carve-out (`TRACE_HEAD=6`, e.g. `do_fork`, `do_sig`, `do_vmctl`, `sched`). CLAUDE.md:136 warns "zero sampled `[ipc]` lines is NOT evidence a caller is stuck." Assert on head-carved `[ksys SYS_FORK]`/`SYS_EXIT`/`SYS_EXEC` and `[as]`/`[alarm]` (reliable), not on tick counts.

## 6. `kernel/build.rs` — hazards for a test harness

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/build.rs`:**
- **Gates on `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS`:** lines 22-25 — `if target_os != "none" { return; }`. On host builds it is a full no-op. **There is NO `CARGO_CFG_TEST` / profile gate.**
- **Consequence for `cargo test --target aarch64-unknown-none`:** `target_os == "none"`, so build.rs runs its **full path**: clang-assembles 5 `.S` files, then `build_boot_image` shells out to **nested `cargo build --release` for all 8 servers** (vm, ds, vfs, sched, rs, pm, init, worker), reads each ELF, packs the MXBI archive, and emits `cargo:rustc-env=BOOT_IMAGE_PATH=...`. This is heavy but would still run; it does not itself break under `--target aarch64-unknown-none`.
- **The real harness blocker is `no_main` + `no_std`:** `main.rs` sets `#![cfg_attr(target_os="none", no_main)]`, and a blog_os-style `custom_test_frameworks` harness needs `#![reexport_test_harness_main = "..."]` plus a `#[no_mangle] _start` calling the generated test main — the kernel already owns `kmain`/`_start` via the linker `ENTRY`. A separate test binary would need its own entry and its own linker handling (the `.cargo/config.toml` `rustflags` force `-Tkernel/.../linker.ld` on every `aarch64-unknown-none` link, including test/bench binaries — build.rs works around this for servers via `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS`, but a `cargo test` integration binary would inherit the kernel linker script).
- Env vars build.rs needs: `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS`, `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_ARCH`, `OUT_DIR`, `CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR`, `CARGO`; requires `clang` on PATH and the `aarch64-unknown-none` target for the nested server builds.

## 7. Existing QEMU/test infrastructure — NONE

Searches for `smoke`, `test_case`, `custom_test_frameworks`, `semihosting`, `psci`, `SYSTEM_OFF`:
- **No matches** for `test_case`, `custom_test_frameworks`, `semihosting`, `psci`, `PSCI`, `SYSTEM_OFF`, `0x84000...` anywhere in the repo.
- `smoke` appears only in prose comments (`arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, `userland.rs`) referring to slice-3.1a's host-side AddrSpace "smoke test," not a QEMU test.
- **No `tests/` directory and no `*.expected` file exist** — the `tests/qemu-boot.expected` in chunk 1 is entirely to-be-created.
- CLAUDE.md:138 confirms "`cargo test -p minixrs-kernel` runs zero tests by design" — kernel modules are all `cfg(target_os="none")`; host-runnable tests live in `kernel-shared` (that's what `miri`/`coverage` jobs exercise).

**Shutdown / idle behavior — the kernel never exits QEMU:**
- `halt() -> !` is a bare `wfe` spin loop (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs:24`). Fallback spins in `entry.S:46` / `vectors.S:61` are also `wfe`.
- Once EL0 starts (slice 2.4+) the scheduler runs **indefinitely**; there is **no PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF`, no semihosting exit, no QEMU `isa-debug-exit`**. CLAUDE.md:37-46: "The kernel runs indefinitely once EL0 starts... so `timeout` is mandatory."
- This validates chunk 1's design: the smoke job must be `timeout 25 cargo run ... > log; grep -a -f expected log` (exit-on-timeout + log grep), **not** a QEMU exit-code assertion. If you later want a clean self-exit for tests you'd have to add a PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF` (HVC `0x84000008`) or `isa-debug-exit`-style path — none exists today.

**Net:** the smoke job is fully greenfield. The kernel already emits stable, greppable kernel-side traces (`[as]`, head-carved `[ksys SYS_FORK/EXEC/EXIT]`, `[alarm]`, panic/`el0_sync_unexpected`) that CLAUDE.md:135 already treats as the manual Phase-4 acceptance check — chunk 1 is essentially codifying that manual check into CI.</result>

**UART path:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/uart.rs` re-exports `crate::arch::Uart`; on aarch64 that is `Pl011` (`arch/aarch64/mod.rs`: `pub use uart::Pl011 as Uart`), MMIO at `PL011_PHYS_BASE` (mapped via HHDM). Output goes to `-serial stdio`.

**Healthy-boot markers (assert presence):**
- `minix.rs booting on aarch64` — very first line (`main.rs:54`).
- `[as] {name} nr= ... ttbr0_pa= ... asid= ... entry=` — one per loaded address space; **11 `[as]` lines** on a good boot (`arch/aarch64/userland.rs:380` servers/init, `:691` stubs). CLAUDE.md:135 confirms: vm/ds/vfs/sched/rs/pm/init asid 1-7, stubs A-D asid 8-11.
- `[ksys SYS_FORK] parent= ... child_nr=15 ...` → `[ksys SYS_EXEC] target=15 name=worker ...` → `[ksys SYS_EXIT] target= ... nr=15 freed= ...` — the init→worker chain (`system/do_fork.rs:191`, `do_exec.rs:171`, `do_exit.rs:125`).
- `[alarm {n}] owner= ... nr= ... at= ...` — RS heartbeat alarm fires (`ipc/mod.rs:390`); CLAUDE.md notes "six RS `[alarm]` fires."
- `[ipc {n}]`, `[pf] proc= ... -> VM`, `[noq {n}]` — other liveness traces (`ipc/mod.rs:125`, `exception.rs:213`, `sched/...:399`).
- All healthy `[ksys …]` traces end `result=0` (CLAUDE.md:135: "every `result=0`").

**Designed (NOT failure) — must be tolerated:** `[ksys SYS_KILL] target= ... sig=11 -> pm` (`system/do_sig.rs:99`) — stub D's intentional SIGSEGV kill chain. The expected file should whitelist `sig=11`.

**Failure markers (assert absence):**
- `!!! KERNEL PANIC:` (`panic.rs:15`).
- `el0_sync_unexpected` / `!!! unexpected EL0 sync exception (not SVC)` (`arch/aarch64/exception.rs:86,114`; also panics with `EL0 sync exception: EC=... ESR_EL1=...` at :122).
- `!!! EL0 instruction abort` / `!!! EL0 data abort` (`exception.rs:95,103`).
- `do_irq: unexpected INTID` (`arch/aarch64/irq.rs:49`).

**Trace sampling caveat (important for choosing assertions):** most traces are *sampled*, not per-call. `[ipc N]` and `[ksys N]` use modulo-100 (`system/mod.rs:78` `KCALL_TRACE_EVERY=100`); several have a head carve-out (`TRACE_HEAD=6`, e.g. `do_fork`, `do_sig`, `do_vmctl`, `sched`). CLAUDE.md:136 warns "zero sampled `[ipc]` lines is NOT evidence a caller is stuck." Assert on head-carved `[ksys SYS_FORK]`/`SYS_EXIT`/`SYS_EXEC` and `[as]`/`[alarm]` (reliable), not on tick counts.

## 6. `kernel/build.rs` — hazards for a test harness

**`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/build.rs`:**
- **Gates on `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS`:** lines 22-25 — `if target_os != "none" { return; }`. On host builds it is a full no-op. **There is NO `CARGO_CFG_TEST` / profile gate.**
- **Consequence for `cargo test --target aarch64-unknown-none`:** `target_os == "none"`, so build.rs runs its **full path**: clang-assembles 5 `.S` files, then `build_boot_image` shells out to **nested `cargo build --release` for all 8 servers** (vm, ds, vfs, sched, rs, pm, init, worker), reads each ELF, packs the MXBI archive, and emits `cargo:rustc-env=BOOT_IMAGE_PATH=...`. This is heavy but would still run; it does not itself break under `--target aarch64-unknown-none`.
- **The real harness blocker is `no_main` + `no_std`:** `main.rs` sets `#![cfg_attr(target_os="none", no_main)]`, and a blog_os-style `custom_test_frameworks` harness needs `#![reexport_test_harness_main = "..."]` plus a `#[no_mangle] _start` calling the generated test main — the kernel already owns `kmain`/`_start` via the linker `ENTRY`. A separate test binary would need its own entry and its own linker handling (the `.cargo/config.toml` `rustflags` force `-Tkernel/.../linker.ld` on every `aarch64-unknown-none` link, including test/bench binaries — build.rs works around this for servers via `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS`, but a `cargo test` integration binary would inherit the kernel linker script).
- Env vars build.rs needs: `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS`, `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_ARCH`, `OUT_DIR`, `CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR`, `CARGO`; requires `clang` on PATH and the `aarch64-unknown-none` target for the nested server builds.

## 7. Existing QEMU/test infrastructure — NONE

Searches for `smoke`, `test_case`, `custom_test_frameworks`, `semihosting`, `psci`, `SYSTEM_OFF`:
- **No matches** for `test_case`, `custom_test_frameworks`, `semihosting`, `psci`, `PSCI`, `SYSTEM_OFF`, `0x84000...` anywhere in the repo.
- `smoke` appears only in prose comments (`arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, `userland.rs`) referring to slice-3.1a's host-side AddrSpace "smoke test," not a QEMU test.
- **No `tests/` directory and no `*.expected` file exist** — the `tests/qemu-boot.expected` in chunk 1 is entirely to-be-created.
- CLAUDE.md:138 confirms "`cargo test -p minixrs-kernel` runs zero tests by design" — kernel modules are all `cfg(target_os="none")`; host-runnable tests live in `kernel-shared` (that's what `miri`/`coverage` jobs exercise).

**Shutdown / idle behavior — the kernel never exits QEMU:**
- `halt() -> !` is a bare `wfe` spin loop (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs:24`). Fallback spins in `entry.S:46` / `vectors.S:61` are also `wfe`.
- Once EL0 starts (slice 2.4+) the scheduler runs **indefinitely**; there is **no PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF`, no semihosting exit, no QEMU `isa-debug-exit`**. CLAUDE.md:37-46: "The kernel runs indefinitely once EL0 starts... so `timeout` is mandatory."
- This validates chunk 1's design: the smoke job must be `timeout 25 cargo run ... > log; grep -a -f expected log` (exit-on-timeout + log grep), **not** a QEMU exit-code assertion. If you later want a clean self-exit for tests you'd have to add a PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF` (HVC `0x84000008`) or `isa-debug-exit`-style path — none exists today.

**Net:** the smoke job is fully greenfield. The kernel already emits stable, greppable kernel-side traces (`[as]`, head-carved `[ksys SYS_FORK/EXEC/EXIT]`, `[alarm]`, panic/`el0_sync_unexpected`) that CLAUDE.md:135 already treats as the manual Phase-4 acceptance check — chunk 1 is essentially codifying that manual check into CI.</result>

The repo survey is in, and it sharpens the picture considerably. The phase-5-prep tracker's chunk 1 already specifies a fairly concrete design: an advisory CI job that boots the kernel under `timeout 25`, captures the serial log, and greps it against a checked-in expected-substrings file — asserting the 11 `[as]` lines, init's fork/exec/exit chain, RS alarms, and absence of panics. Two load-bearing facts for the blog_os question:

1. **The kernel never exits QEMU** — no PSCI SYSTEM_OFF, no semihosting, nothing. A blog_os-style framework needs a QEMU exit primitive, so that would be new kernel code.
2. **A `cargo test` harness has real friction here**: `.cargo/config.toml` forces the kernel linker script onto every aarch64-unknown-none link (test binaries included), the kernel owns `_start`/`kmain` via linker ENTRY, and build.rs would run its full 8-server nested build for every test binary. The cfg-gating in main.rs is per-item across ~10 modules, and its purpose (host `cargo check`/`clippy` compatibility) wouldn't be removed by a QEMU-side test framework — the boot log grep approach codifies exactly the manual Phase-4 acceptance check that already exists.

Still waiting on the third agent researching aarch64 semihosting exit mechanics, custom_test_frameworks' current nightly status, and GitHub Actions ARM runner feasibility — that will settle how viable the blog_os path is before I bring you options.

# Research report: CI smoke/test harness for an aarch64 bare-metal Rust kernel

## 1. Exiting `qemu-system-aarch64` with a controllable exit code from EL1

### Semihosting (recommended, works cleanly for nonzero codes)

**Instruction/register convention (A64, not arm32):** On AArch64 the semihosting trap instruction is `HLT #0xF000` (arm32 uses `SVC 0x123456` in ARM state or `SVC 0xAB` in Thumb; do NOT use those on A64). The call number goes in `W0`/`X0`, the argument (usually a pointer to a parameter block) in `X1`. For exit: `W0 = 0x18` (`SYS_EXIT` / `angel_SWIreason_ReportException`).

**Key finding — aarch64 supports nonzero exit codes natively, no `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED` needed.** This is the important A64-vs-arm32 distinction confirmed in QEMU source (`semihosting/arm-compat-semi.c`):

- On **A64**, `SYS_EXIT`'s argument (`X1`) is a *pointer to a two-word parameter block* `[reason, subcode]`. QEMU logic: if `reason == ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit (0x20026)`, it exits the host process with `subcode` directly (any value); otherwise it exits with `1`. Source comment: *"The A64 version of SYS_EXIT takes a parameter block, so the application-exit type can return a subcode which is the exit status code from the application."*
- On **A32/T32**, `SYS_EXIT` passes only the reason code in the register (no pointer), so you can only get exit 0 (if `ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit`) or 1. To return an arbitrary code on arm32 you'd need the optional `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED (0x20)`. **On aarch64 you do not need `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED`** — the ordinary `SYS_EXIT` extended (pointer) form already carries the code.

So the exit code maps **directly** to the host process exit status (masked to 0–255 by POSIX). This is cleaner than blog_os's x86 `isa-debug-exit`, which needs the `(value<<1)|1` remapping (blog_os sets `test-success-exit-code = 33`). On aarch64, `exit(0)` → host 0 (cargo test sees success), `exit(1)` → host 1, etc. No remapping required.

The historical bug where arm semihosting *"always calls exit(0)"* ([Launchpad #1216845](https://bugs.launchpad.net/qemu/+bug/1216845)) was fixed via the semihosting v2 ABI (merged QEMU 4.2). Any modern QEMU (Ubuntu 24.04 ships 8.2.2) is far past this.

**QEMU flags:** Enable with `-semihosting` or `-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native`. The `target` matters for exit-code propagation:
- `target=native` (the default when no debugger, via `target=auto`): QEMU handles the call itself and the exit code reaches the host process. **Use this.**
- `target=gdb`: calls are forwarded to a connected gdb stub — you do NOT want this for CI, the host exit status won't reflect the guest.

You can add `chardev`/output redirect, but for exit that's not needed.

**Gotchas:**
- **Security:** QEMU docs are explicit — *"Semihosting inherently bypasses any isolation there may be between the guest and the host"* and *"you should only ever run trusted code with semihosting enabled."* Enabling it lets the guest read/write host files, `SYS_READC` can block forever, etc. Irrelevant for trusted CI, but note it.
- **Semihosting only works under TCG, not KVM.** QEMU docs: the trap instructions are reserved and *"most hypervisors trap and fault on them,"* so semihosting is TCG-only. This matters for point 3 below (you can't KVM-accelerate a kernel that exits via semihosting).
- **`-semihosting` changes the meaning of instructions.** With it enabled, `HLT #0xF000` (and on arm32 the semihosting `SVC`/`BKPT` immediates) are interpreted as semihosting calls instead of trapping normally. If your kernel legitimately uses `HLT #0xF000`, behavior changes. In practice fine.
- **Endianness edge case (flag as low-risk):** For a big-endian aarch64 guest on a LE host, `exit(0)` currently comes back as host `1` because the reason word's bytes are swapped and no longer match `0x20026` ([QEMU gitlab #3258](https://gitlab.com/qemu-project/qemu/-/issues/3258)). Does NOT affect a normal little-endian kernel.

### Alternative: PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF` via HVC on `-M virt`

`-M virt` emulates PSCI. From EL1 you load `X0 = 0x84000008` (`PSCI_SYSTEM_OFF`, the 32-bit function ID) and execute `HVC #0` (QEMU virt handles PSCI at EL2/EL3 via HVC/SMC). This is a *clean* shutdown, but **always yields host exit code 0** — there's no way to pass a status. So it's fine for a "kernel booted and reached end cleanly" smoke test, but useless for signaling test *failure*. Practical pattern: PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF` on success, and rely on a timeout / semihosting exit(nonzero) / panic for failure. Given you want a controllable code, semihosting is the better primary mechanism.

### Is there an `isa-debug-exit` equivalent on `virt`? — No

`isa-debug-exit` (`hw/misc/debugexit.c`, the `(N<<1)|1` device blog_os uses) is an **ISA/port-I/O device, x86-only**. The aarch64 `virt` machine has **no** `isa-debug-exit` and no general `sysbus-debug-exit` equivalent exposed on `virt`. (There is ongoing work on a `virt-ctrl`/`goldfish`-style reset controller in Linux, but nothing you'd rely on for this.) The idiomatic aarch64 answer is semihosting `SYS_EXIT`; that's exactly what the Rust crates below do.

### `-no-reboot` / `-no-shutdown` interplay

- Semihosting `SYS_EXIT` terminates the QEMU process immediately, so `-no-reboot`/`-no-shutdown` don't affect that path.
- They matter for the *failure/crash* path: if a panic/exception triggers a CPU reset loop, `-no-reboot` makes QEMU exit instead of rebooting forever (avoids a hung CI job). `-no-shutdown` does the opposite (keeps QEMU alive after a guest-initiated shutdown) — you generally do **not** want `-no-shutdown` in CI. Recommended: add `-no-reboot` as a safety net alongside a workflow-level `timeout`.

## 2. `custom_test_frameworks` status on 2025/2026 nightlies

- **Still available and unstable**, gated by `#![feature(custom_test_frameworks)]`. Tracking issue is [rust-lang/rust#50297](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/50297) (eRFC 2318), still **open**; the Unstable Book entry still documents `#[test_case]` + `#![test_runner(...)]` with no deprecation notice.
- **No deprecation/removal in progress.** The feature has been quietly unstable since ~2018 with *"currently no plan/RFC for stabilizing"* — i.e. stable and long-lived precisely because nobody's touching it. No removal discussion found.
- The full blog_os trio still works on `no_std`/`no_main`:
  ```rust
  #![no_std]
  #![no_main]
  #![feature(custom_test_frameworks)]
  #![test_runner(crate::test_runner)]
  #![reexport_test_harness_main = "test_main"]
  ```
  `reexport_test_harness_main` renames the generated harness entry (e.g. to `test_main`) so your `_start` can call it. This is exactly blog_os's [testing chapter](https://os.phil-opp.com/testing/) approach; it transfers to aarch64 by swapping the x86 `isa-debug-exit` `exit_qemu()` for a semihosting `SYS_EXIT`.
- Caveat: because it's a permanently-unstable feature, you must stay on nightly — which you already are (pinned dated nightly). No stable path exists.

## 3. GitHub Actions runners

### `ubuntu-24.04-arm` (native arm64) hosted runners

- **Free for public repos.** Linux arm64 hosted runners (`ubuntu-22.04-arm`, `ubuntu-24.04-arm`) went to public preview and are **free on public repositories, including the Free plan** ([GitHub changelog, 2025-01-16](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-01-16-linux-arm64-hosted-runners-now-available-for-free-in-public-repositories-public-preview/); [community discussion #148648](https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/148648)). 4 vCPU, Cobalt-100 / Armv9-A (SVE2). **These labels fail on private repos** (private-repo arm64 is billed/larger-runner only).
- **No `/dev/kvm` on arm64 runners.** Confirmed by [actions/partner-runner-images #147 "Please support KVM on ARM runners"](https://github.com/actions/partner-runner-images/issues/147): `/dev/kvm` is absent; attempts give *"Could not access KVM kernel module: No such file or directory."* GitHub hosted runners don't do nested virtualization. **So `qemu-system-aarch64 -M virt` will run under TCG even on the arm64 runner** (and recall semihosting is TCG-only anyway, so you couldn't use `-accel kvm` with the semihosting-exit mechanism regardless). This is fine for a smoke test.
- **QEMU version:** Ubuntu 24.04 (noble) ships `qemu-system-arm` **1:8.2.2** (QEMU 8.2.2, with `-0ubuntu1.x` security updates). More than new enough — aarch64 `-M virt` semihosting exit codes have worked since QEMU 4.2. Basically "any modern QEMU."

### `ubuntu-latest` (x86) runners

- `qemu-system-aarch64` there runs the aarch64 guest under **TCG** (an x86 KVM host cannot accelerate an aarch64 guest). `/dev/kvm` on standard x86 runners is inconsistent ("sometimes exists"; larger runners can enable it) but **irrelevant** here since it wouldn't accelerate a cross-arch guest anyway.
- **TCG slowdown:** typically ~5–20x slower than native per the [TCG Continuous Benchmarking](https://ahmedkrmn.github.io/TCG-Continuous-Benchmarking/Measuring-QEMU-Performance-in-System-Mode/) project, but for a bare-metal kernel *smoke* test (boot to a few test cases then exit) this is a sub-second-to-few-seconds workload — TCG is completely adequate. Many Rust OS projects (blog_os and the broader rust-osdev ecosystem, [rust-osdev-jumpstart](https://github.com/alilee/rust-osdev-jumpstart) which does x86_64/arm/riscv under TCG) run boot tests this way successfully in CI.

**Recommendation:** Use `ubuntu-24.04-arm` (free, native toolchain, apt QEMU 8.2.2, TCG for the guest) OR just `ubuntu-latest` x86 with cross-compiled aarch64 kernel under TCG. Both work; neither gets hardware acceleration for the guest. Add `-no-reboot` and a job `timeout-minutes`.

## 4. Prior art / crates

### `qemu-exit` (rust-embedded, formerly andre-richter)

- **Supports aarch64:** yes — `qemu_exit::AArch64::new()`, then `.exit_success()` / `.exit_failure()` / `.exit(code)`.
- **Exact mechanism (from source `src/aarch64.rs`):** executes `hlt #0xF000` with `x0 = 0x18` (SYS_EXIT) and `x1` → a `#[repr(C)]` two-`u64` parameter block `{ ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit = 0x20026, code }`. QEMU uses the second field as the host exit code (exactly the mechanism in §1). `EXIT_SUCCESS = 0`, `EXIT_FAILURE = 1`. Requires `-semihosting`.
- **License:** dual **MIT OR Apache-2.0** — permissive, no GPL. Fine.
- **Maintenance:** owned by the rust-embedded libs team (Adam Greig, Andre Richter). Mature/stable rather than actively churning; small crate, ~93 commits, does one thing. Widely used in aarch64 tutorials (e.g. rust-raspberrypi-OS-tutorials).

### `semihosting` (taiki-e) — the more modern, actively-maintained option

- **Targets:** AArch64 (`aarch64-unknown-none{,-softfloat}`, plus `aarch64_be`), ARM, RISC-V, LoongArch, MIPS, Xtensa — tested under both qemu-system and qemu-user.
- **Exit:** `semihosting::process::exit(code)` / `abort()` — same `HLT #0xF000` + `SYS_EXIT` ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit path; host exit code follows `code` (subject to the big-endian caveat noted in §1, which doesn't affect LE kernels).
- **`no_std`, feature-gated extras:** `stdio` (host stdout/stderr, useful to print test names), `panic-handler` / `panic-unwind`, `args`, `backtrace`, plus `trap-hlt` and `openocd-semihosting` knobs. There's a companion `panic-semihosting`-style behavior (panic → log to host stderr → `exit(EXIT_FAILURE)`), which pairs perfectly with a custom `test_runner`.
- **License:** dual **MIT OR Apache-2.0** — permissive, no GPL.
- **Maintenance:** active — ~476 commits, ongoing CI across all those targets, open issues/PRs being handled. If you want a maintained, richer crate (stdout for test output + exit + panic in one), this is the stronger choice over `qemu-exit`.

### `cortex-m-semihosting` equivalent

`cortex-m-semihosting` is Cortex-M (armv7-m/thumb) only and not applicable to A64 EL1. The AArch64 equivalents are the two crates above; **taiki-e/semihosting is effectively the cross-arch successor** to the older per-arch semihosting crates.

---

## Bottom line / recommended design

- Exit mechanism: **semihosting `SYS_EXIT` (`HLT #0xF000`, `W0=0x18`, `X1`→`[0x20026, code]`)** with QEMU `-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native`. Host exit code == your code, no remapping, nonzero codes work natively on aarch64. Use the **`semihosting` (taiki-e)** crate (or `qemu-exit`); both MIT/Apache-2.0.
- Test harness: keep blog_os's `custom_test_frameworks` + `reexport_test_harness_main` (still available/unstable on nightly, no deprecation), just replace the x86 `isa-debug-exit` `exit_qemu()` with the semihosting exit. Success = exit(0), failure = exit(nonzero) or panic→exit(1).
- No `isa-debug-exit`/`sysbus-debug-exit` on `-M virt`; PSCI `SYSTEM_OFF` (HVC, `X0=0x84000008`) only gives exit 0, so use it (optionally) for clean success but not for failure signaling.
- CI: `ubuntu-24.04-arm` (free on public repos, QEMU 8.2.2) or `ubuntu-latest` x86; guest runs under **TCG** either way (no `/dev/kvm` on GitHub arm runners; semihosting is TCG-only regardless). Add `-no-reboot` and a `timeout-minutes` guard.

### Sources

- [QEMU Emulation/semihosting docs](https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/about/emulation.html)
- [QEMU `semihosting/arm-compat-semi.c` (A64 SYS_EXIT param block logic)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/qemu/qemu/master/semihosting/arm-compat-semi.c)
- [QEMU `hw/misc/debugexit.c` (isa-debug-exit, x86-only)](https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/master/hw/misc/debugexit.c)
- [Launchpad #1216845 — arm semihosting always exit(0), fixed via SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED / v2 ABI](https://bugs.launchpad.net/qemu/+bug/1216845)
- [QEMU gitlab #3258 — big-endian semihosting exit code](https://gitlab.com/qemu-project/qemu/-/issues/3258)
- [cirosantilli linux-kernel-module-cheat aarch64 semihost_exit.S](https://github.com/cirosantilli/linux-kernel-module-cheat/blob/master/baremetal/arch/aarch64/semihost_exit.S)
- [Löwenware — AArch64 bare-metal Rust, PSCI SYSTEM_OFF via HVC](https://lowenware.com/blog/aarch64-bare-metal-program-in-rust/)
- [rust-lang/rust#50297 — custom_test_frameworks tracking issue](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/50297)
- [Unstable Book — custom-test-frameworks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/unstable-book/language-features/custom-test-frameworks.html)
- [blog_os — Testing (custom_test_frameworks, isa-debug-exit, reexport_test_harness_main)](https://os.phil-opp.com/testing/)
- [GitHub changelog — free arm64 runners for public repos (2025-01-16)](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-01-16-linux-arm64-hosted-runners-now-available-for-free-in-public-repositories-public-preview/)
- [community discussion #148648 — arm64 runners free public preview](https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/148648)
- [actions/partner-runner-images #147 — KVM not available on ARM runners](https://github.com/actions/partner-runner-images/issues/147)
- [community discussion #8305 — KVM on hosted runners](https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/8305)
- [Ubuntu Noble qemu-system-arm 8.2.2 package](https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/noble/arm64/qemu-system-arm)
- [TCG Continuous Benchmarking — system-mode perf](https://ahmedkrmn.github.io/TCG-Continuous-Benchmarking/Measuring-QEMU-Performance-in-System-Mode/)
- [qemu-exit (rust-embedded) — MIT/Apache-2.0, aarch64](https://github.com/rust-embedded/qemu-exit) and [aarch64 source](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rust-embedded/qemu-exit/master/src/aarch64.rs)
- [taiki-e/semihosting — MIT/Apache-2.0, aarch64 + more](https://github.com/taiki-e/semihosting) / [crates.io](https://crates.io/crates/semihosting)

Uncertainties flagged: exact `/dev/kvm` presence on *standard x86* runners varies over time but is moot for a cross-arch aarch64 guest (always TCG); the big-endian exit(0)→1 quirk only affects `aarch64_be` guests; `custom_test_frameworks` has no deprecation *today* but is a permanently-unstable feature, so it will keep you on nightly indefinitely.</result>
